era_context: The Roman Empire under Diocletian is in the early phase of radical administrative and military reorganization. Diocletian has ruled as sole Augustus since 284 CE, consolidating power after the civil wars of the 3rd century. In 286 CE, he elevates Maximian to Caesar (and possibly Augustus, depending on source tradition), marking the beginning of the Tetrarchic system. The empire faces multiple crises: usurpers, Germanic tribes on the Rhine and Danube, and Sassanid Persia in the East. Christianity remains persecuted but growing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese sources may exist but underrepresented)

events.title: Diocletian appoints Maximian as Caesar; beginning of the Tetrarchy
events.description: Diocletian appoints Maximian, his trusted general, to the rank of Caesar and assigns him responsibility for the Western Roman Empire. This marks the formal beginning of the Tetrarchic system, though the system will not be fully operational until 293 CE when a second Caesar is appointed in the East. Maximian is tasked with consolidating the Western Empire, suppressing usurpers, and defending the Rhine frontier. Some sources elevate Maximian directly to Augustus in 286 CE, while others place this elevation in 287 CE. The appointment of Maximian is a crucial step in Diocletian's administrative reforms.
events.certainty_note: The appointment of Maximian is well-attested in multiple later sources. The date of 286 CE is widely accepted, though some sources place his elevation to Augustus in 287 CE.

events.title: Carausius declares himself emperor of Britain
events.description: Marcus Aurelius Mausaeus Carausius, a Roman naval commander of Menapian origin, declares himself emperor and establishes an independent state in Britain. He controls the British Isles and parts of northern Gaul (the Menapi territory). Carausius mints coins with the legend 'RSR' (Restitutor Britanniae) and maintains Roman administrative structures. His usurpation is a significant challenge to Diocletian and Maximian's authority. The exact dating of Carausius's usurpation is debated (some sources place it in 286 CE, others in 287–290 CE), but 286 CE is within the accepted range.
key_figures: Carausius (Roman naval officer, self-proclaimed emperor of Britain)
events.certainty_note: Carausius's usurpation is confirmed by contemporary coins. The exact year is debated; 286–290 CE are all plausible based on different source traditions. Some scholars place the usurpation earlier (286 CE), others later (288–290 CE).

events.title: Diocletian's administrative reforms continue; provincial reorganization
events.description: Diocletian continues implementing his sweeping administrative and military reforms across the empire. These reforms include the division of provinces into smaller administrative units, the reorganization of the military into mobile field armies and frontier garrisons, and the centralization of tax collection. The appointment of Maximian as Caesar is part of this broader reorganization. By 286 CE, these reforms are well underway, though their full implementation will take several more years.
events.certainty_note: Diocletian's reforms are well-attested in later sources and by their long-term effects. The specific timeline for 286 CE is less certain, but the reforms were ongoing during this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating of Maximian's appointment and elevation is disputed. Some sources place his appointment as Caesar in 286 CE and his elevation to Augustus in 287 CE; others place both events in 286 CE or 287 CE. The dating of Carausius's usurpation is similarly debated, with scholars proposing dates ranging from 286 to 290 CE. Some modern scholars argue that the Tetrarchic system was not fully formalized until 293 CE, making the significance of 286 CE as a 'beginning' debatable.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 286 CE is sparse and heavily dependent on later sources written in the 4th century, often with propagandistic intent. Contemporary documentation is minimal. Numismatic evidence (coins) provides some grounding for Carausius's rule. The perspective is heavily Western-Roman; Eastern sources are limited. Archaeological evidence from frontier sites and administrative centres provides indirect confirmation but rarely precise dating. The Tetrarchic system is well-attested in later sources, but the exact chronology of its establishment is debated among modern scholars. The reforms attributed to Diocletian are often described in retrospect, making precise dating difficult.
